full_response:
Let  

 G = (V ,E) |V| = n, |E| = m  

Every edge e ∈ E carries  
 weight-limit w(e) ∈ ℝ_{>0}  
 cost    c(e) ∈ ℝ_{>0}

Goal (lexicographic):

1. maximise bottleneck(T)   with bottleneck(T) = min { w(e) | e ∈ T }  
   over all spanning trees T of G; call the optimum value B\*.  
2. among all spanning trees whose bottleneck is B\*, minimise  
    cost(T)= Σ_{e∈T} c(e).

We must output such an optimal tree in O(m log n) time.



────────────────────────────────────────
Algorithm
────────────────────────────────────────
We solve the two objectives one after the other.

STEP 1 (find the best possible bottleneck B*)  
 • sort the edges once in non-increasing order of weight-limit  
  (e_{1},e_{2},…,e_{m}) with w(e_{1}) ≥ w(e_{2}) ≥ ⋯ ≥ w(e_{m}).  
 • run Kruskal’s union–find in that order until the graph becomes
   connected for the first time.

```
Bottleneck-Kruskal(E):
    make-set(v) for every v ∈ V
    components ← n
    for e in edges sorted by decreasing w(e):
        E'  ← E' ∪ {e}          // store – will be needed in step 2
        if find(e.u) ≠ find(e.v):
            union(e.u,e.v)
            components ← components – 1
            if components = 1:          // connectivity achieved
                B ← w(e)                // present edge is the minimum
                break                   // in the set just added
    // add the remaining edges whose weight is still ≥ B
    for remaining e in list while w(e) ≥ B:
         E' ← E' ∪ {e}
    return (B , E')
```

At termination (B ,E′) are produced, where

 E′ = { e ∈ E | w(e) ≥ B }.                 …(1)

Run time of the whole step: sorting O(m log m)=O(m log n),  
union–find O(m α(n)) ⊆ O(m log n).

STEP 2 (minimum cost inside the admissible edges)  
Compute a minimum–cost spanning tree of the graph (V ,E′) using any
O(|E′| log n) MST algorithm (Kruskal by cost or Prim with heaps).

```
Cost-MST(V ,E', c):
    sort E' in non-decreasing cost
    run normal Kruskal to obtain tree T*
    return T*
```

Total running time = O(m log n).



────────────────────────────────────────
Correctness Proof
────────────────────────────────────────
Lemma 1.  
The value B returned by Step 1 is the maximum possible bottleneck B\*.

Proof.  
Consider the prefix P of the sorted edge list processed until the
moment connectivity was first achieved.  
All edges in P have weight ≥ B and P contains a spanning tree
(by construction).  
Suppose for contradiction that there exists a spanning tree T with
bottleneck strictly larger than B.  
All edges of T therefore appear earlier than the edge of weight B,
hence are contained in P.  
But immediately before B was processed, the union–find still had more
than one component, meaning P was not yet connected,
hence cannot contain T – contradiction. ∎



Lemma 2.  
After Step 1, E′ satisfies (1) and hence contains every edge that can
appear in any spanning tree whose bottleneck is at least B.

Immediate from definition of E′. ∎



Lemma 3.  
The tree T* produced in Step 2 minimises total cost among all spanning
trees with bottleneck ≥ B.

Proof.  
By Lemma 2 every such spanning tree lives in the graph (V ,E′).
Step 2 runs a standard minimum-cost spanning-tree algorithm on
(V ,E′), whose correctness is classical, so T* has minimum possible
cost among all spanning trees of that graph, hence among all spanning
trees with bottleneck ≥ B. ∎



Theorem.  
T* returned by the algorithm fulfils both optimisation criteria.

Proof.  
By Lemma 1 the algorithm’s B equals B\*, the optimum bottleneck.
By Lemma 3 tree T* is the cheapest spanning tree whose bottleneck is
at least B\*; no other spanning tree can have bottleneck larger than
B\* (optimality of B\*), so T* is optimal for the lexicographic
objective. ∎



────────────────────────────────────────
Complexity Analysis
────────────────────────────────────────
• Sorting by weight limits: O(m log n)  
• Union–Find operations:  O(m α(n)) ⊆ O(m log n)  
• Sorting E′ by cost:    O(|E′| log n) ≤ O(m log n)  
Overall time: O(m log n)

Space: O(m + n) for edge list, union–find and MST.



────────────────────────────────────────
Pseudocode (full)
────────────────────────────────────────
```
OptimalBridges(V ,E):
    // ---------- Step 1 ----------
    sort E decreasing by w
    make-set(v) for v in V
    components ← |V|
    E' ← ∅
    for e in E in that order:
        if w(e) < w(E[0]): pass       // nothing special
        E' ← E' ∪ {e}
        if find(e.u) ≠ find(e.v):
            union(e.u,e.v); components--
            if components = 1:
                 B ← w(e); break
    for remaining e while w(e) ≥ B:
         E' ← E' ∪ {e}

    // ---------- Step 2 ----------
    sort E' increasing by c
    T* ← ∅
    make-set(v) for v in V
    for e in E' in that order:
        if find(e.u) ≠ find(e.v):
            union(e.u,e.v)
            T* ← T* ∪ {e}
            if |T*| = |V|−1: return T*
```

────────────────────────────────────────
\boxed{ Sort\ edges\ by\ weight\ \downarrow,\ run\ Kruskal\ until\ connected\; the
smallest\ weight\ seen\ is\ the\ optimal\ bottleneck\ B^{\*}.
Restrict\ to\ edges\ of\ weight\ \ge B^{\*}\ and\ run\ any\ 
minimum-cost\ spanning-tree\ algorithm.
Total\ time\ O(|E|\log|V|). }

Confidence: 90%
